And I think, maybe, my sadness was part of the reason she answered the next time I called.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div class=""></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">Two years later, Terese and I prepared to graduate from Prince George’s Community College. I’d cross the stage as an Honors Academy scholar, a program that provided its members seamless transfer to several local universities, including Howard, the historically black university in Washington. I was completing an application, expecting to be a Howard Bison, until I got to this question: “Have you ever been adjudicated guilty or convicted of a misdemeanor, felony or other crime?” The question made me stop. I feared that my enrollment was already in jeopardy. A few weeks later, Dr. Melinda Frederick, then the coordinator of the Honors Academy, and I went to Howard to sign my enrollment papers. An admissions officer paused when we told her about my felonies. She told us that in the past, Howard had at times declined to admit students who had criminal records. “But don’t worry,” she said. “We’ll get back to you shortly.” They never did. Recently, more than a decade later, I called to find out what happened. Howard says its records list my application as incomplete. When I asked if there were a policy to decline admissions to people with criminal records, a university spokeswoman said that applications by people with criminal records are evaluated on a case-by-case basis.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">I applied to the University of Maryland and was admitted with a full-tuition academic scholarship. Terese, pregnant with our first son, Micah Michael-Zamir, was accepted at Towson State University. After my two years at Maryland, I was chosen by a group of deans and administrators to give our graduation’s student commencement address. Before more than 15,000 people — classmates, professors, friends and family members — I told the audience that I had served eight years and three months in prison. I made my criminal record, even in the middle of an accomplishment, visible — brutally permanent. A tattoo. That’s how I saw it. And if I was going to be scarred, I wanted to reveal it myself.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div class=""><div class="css-79elbk" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper"><div class="css-z3e15g" data-testid="photoviewer-wrapper-hidden"></div><div data-testid="photoviewer-children" class="css-1a48zt4 e11si9ry5"><figure class="img-sz-medium css-3mi1lt e1g7ppur0" aria-label="media" role="group"><div class="css-1xdhyk6 erfvjey0"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Image</span><img alt="The writer with his wife, Terese, and their sons, Miles (left) and Micah, while they were in Brooklyn visiting friends." class="css-r3fift" src="http://web.archive.org/web/20211230230233im_/https://static01.nyt.com/images/2018/10/21/magazine/21GettingOut1/21GettingOut1-articleLarge.png?quality=75&amp;auto=webp&amp;disable=upscale" srcset="http://web.archive.org/web/20211230230233im_/https://static01.nyt.com/images/2018/10/21/magazine/21GettingOut1/21GettingOut1-articleLarge.png?quality=75&amp;auto=webp 600w, http://web.archive.org/web/20211230230233im_/https://static01.nyt.com/images/2018/10/21/magazine/21GettingOut1/21GettingOut1-jumbo.png?quality=75&amp;auto=webp 683w, http://web.archive.org/web/20211230230233im_/https://static01.nyt.com/images/2018/10/21/magazine/21GettingOut1/21GettingOut1-superJumbo.png?quality=75&amp;auto=webp 1365w" sizes="((min-width: 600px) and (max-width: 1004px)) 84vw, (min-width: 1005px) 60vw, 100vw" decoding="async" width="600" height="900"/></div><figcaption class="css-1l44abu ewdxa0s0"><span aria-hidden="true" class="css-i48y28 e13ogyst0">The writer with his wife, Terese, and their sons, Miles (left) and Micah, while they were in Brooklyn visiting friends.</span><span class="css-ach9cc e1z0qqy90"><span class="css-1ly73wi e1tej78p0">Credit...</span><span>Mamadi Doumbouya for The New York Times</span></span></figcaption></figure></div></div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Before my 30th</strong> birthday, I’d earned a bachelor’s degree and an M.F.A. in poetry; published “A Question of Freedom,” a memoir about my time in prison; published a collection of poetry, “Shahid Reads His Own Palm”; and still knew my state number by heart. I applied for just about every teaching gig at colleges and private schools in the Washington area without getting a single interview. Peers without records — and some without publications — were getting jobs, or at least interviews. My first job in prison, washing dishes, sweeping and mopping floors in the kitchen, paid 23 cents an hour. Some days I felt I had a better chance of getting that job back than teaching poetry at a nearby school. I stretched my job search to all of the United States. Terese, completing her master’s degree in occupational therapy, was pregnant with our second son, Miles Thelonious, who was born on Oct. 10, 2011. And I was gravid with fear: unemployed and too embarrassed to admit to Terese that I worried I’d never be gainfully employed, able to pay the rent or purchase diapers.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">Then one day in March 2011, the director of Harvard’s Radcliffe Institute for Advanced Study left a phone message offering me a Radcliffe fellowship to finish working on the collection of poems that would become “Bastards of the Reagan Era,” published in 2015. I listened to the message five times, elated and disbelieving. Applying for the fellowship had been a Hail Mary. And then we were moving to Massachusetts, 300 miles away from the only family we knew, a little boy barely potty-trained in tow and another on the way.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">About a year later, apprehensive about trying and failing to get a teaching job, I decided to apply to law school. I figured that at least for three years, my student loan bill wouldn’t be due each month. The first sentence of my personal statement read, “The part of my life that has been most influential in my drive to go to law school is also the greatest obstacle to my being admitted to law school and becoming an attorney.”</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">By April 2013, I’d been accepted to law schools at Yale, Harvard, Columbia, the University of Michigan, Georgetown, the University of Pennsylvania, Northwestern and Boston College; told yes by one school for every year I’d been in prison. Before I was admitted to Northwestern, Clifford Zimmerman, then dean of students, called me. He asked me to tell him something that wasn’t on my résumé. We talked about the book club for 6-to-17-year-old black boys, YoungMenRead, that I started while at Karibu Books; about my sons; about Terese. We talked about the character and fitness challenges I might confront later. He didn’t ask about prison, though he did give me the names of lawyers in three states who might assist me when it came time to be admitted to the bar. Even before I began law school, I knew what was ahead of me.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div class=""></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">I enrolled at Yale Law School just shy of my 33rd birthday. During admitted-students weekend, the dean, Robert Post, gave my 1L class a speech. In our number, he said, was a Cordon Bleu chef, military officers, a poet — me. He didn’t say that among us was a person formerly incarcerated, a felon, an ex-con — he said “poet.” I imagined that I’d begun to outpace the worst of my past. Until one day Supreme Court Justice Sonia Sotomayor came to Yale for a public conversation with the former New York Times legal correspondent Linda Greenhouse. During the discussion, Greenhouse asked why the term “undocumented immigrant” was more accurate than “illegal alien.” Justice Sotomayor replied that “many of these people are people I know, and they are no different than the people I grew up with or who share my life. And they are human beings with a serious legal problem — but the word ‘illegal alien’ made them sound like those other kinds of criminals. And I think people then paint those individuals as something less than worthy human beings.” My classmates applauded. I sank into the chair, unable to stretch my legs, like a man constrained by shackles — understanding that for many people, I was one of “those other kinds of criminals.”</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">A few weeks after the Sotomayor event, Micah’s kindergarten teacher told me that one of his classmates, having overheard a conversation between his parents, had told Micah that I’d stolen a car and went to jail. He and Miles, who was 2, were at a local private school. Terese and I had grappled with what to tell their teachers about my past. We opted for silence, maybe without ever really agreeing on it. Micah’s teacher told me that he had cried and was visibly upset — but he was now O.K. And I couldn’t help wondering what “now O.K.” meant.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">That afternoon, when Micah came home, we sat at the dining room table to talk. Prison has always been the distance between the world and me, but that distance didn’t matter until I realized it also became the distance between me and my sons. Terese and I had never discussed <em class="css-2fg4z9 e1gzwzxm0">when</em> we might tell them — yet we expected to dictate that when. But we hadn’t. Everything Micah had known about me had collapsed into a word: <em class="css-2fg4z9 e1gzwzxm0">jail</em>. I didn’t know if I was O.K. but was certain that he couldn’t be. <em class="css-2fg4z9 e1gzwzxm0">Micah, this is what happened. </em>And I explained, though not everything. Instead of a pistol and a man being told to “get the [expletive] out” of the car and then prison and the rest, it was just: I stole a car and went to jail. He asked me how long. When I told him eight years, I could see in his eyes that he was struggling with what it meant for me to have been in prison longer than he’d been alive. Eight years. “But don’t bad people go to jail, Daddy?” Micah’s voice sounded like the air whistling out of a balloon. I was a first-year law student, explaining how prison, how crime, was never just about being bad. I also recognized that conversations about criminal-justice reform and the new Jim Crow were convenient ways to avoid admitting I’d pulled a gun on a man without a good reason. I wondered if there was room for me to escape being characterized as bad by the 6-year-old boy who first made me feel free.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">The morning of</strong> law-school commencement in May 2016, my classmates and I gathered with thousands of students in the center of Yale’s campus. It was just over 19 years to the date of my sentencing. I carried the law-school flag, leading the graduates from that yard to the college’s Old Campus. We walked the old colonial streets, crossing Elm, making a left turn onto High Street. From somewhere in the crowd, my cousin Reds watched, seeing me lead the procession in a Yale blue gown with a purple hood draped across my back. Arrested at age 14, he had recently been released after 15 years in prison, about two decades before his original sentence was set to finish. His early release was unexpected, mercy from a judge whose reasons I cannot begin to divine.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">But what do you do with a second chance that no one prepared you for? No prison officials would have thought it their responsibility to teach Reds anything more than standing for count. When he came home at age 29, tattoos adorned his body, and he had long dreadlocks that he sheared to appear more employable. He had participated in the job-training programs suggested by his probation officer. He had learned to write a résumé, though 15 years of prison gives little to seduce an employer. Nothing worked. During the next two years, he would be denied dozens of jobs. Job applications became a wall preventing him from ever speaking to a person with the authority to hire him, from having the opportunity to explain that he was more than his crime and time in prison. He teetered on the verge of homelessness. I watched and didn’t do nearly enough.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">What Reds needed most — time to both fail and grow — no one was willing to offer him. I was graduating from one of the best law schools in the world. Still, I was worried that despite my degree, I’d be prevented from practicing law. And so our situations were similar and dissimilar; though it seems peculiar to suggest that graduating with a Yale law degree leaves anyone, no matter how many felonies he has, in a situation as desperate as Reds’s.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">When I was</strong> given J.’s case, a few months after my graduation, he had been incarcerated for a month. J. was already 18, and the charges he faced were serious enough that a long prison sentence was a possibility. The only information I had about J.’s case was a video of police officers interviewing him; another one of officers interviewing the victim, a kid my client’s age, and his mother; and a few charging documents. I watched the video of the officers’ interviewing my client; J. leaned his plastic chair against a wall, his arms tucked into his sweatshirt, the room freezing. For an hour, he said nothing. In the interview with the kid who’d been robbed and his mother, with each detail of the supposed crime, she would say: “I told you about hanging around those devils. I told you about hanging around those devils.” Later, the officers brought out a photo of my client. Is this him? they asked. “Let me see that picture,” the mother said. After a pause, her voice dropped: “He could be my son.”</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div class=""></div><div class=""><div style="max-width:100%;margin:0 auto" class=""><div data-id="100000006159483" data-slug="21mag-gettingout-pullquote3" class="css-17dprlf" style="max-width:600px"><iframe src="http://web.archive.org/web/20211230230233if_/https://www.nytimes.com/interactive/2021/uri/embeddedinteractive/28e28fba-fb85-5131-9187-b9905d892778" class="css-sqdgy8" frameborder="0" scrolling="no" width="100%"></iframe></div></div></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">I understood what she meant. The way it was easy for a bad decision to transform any of the black boys around us from students into victims or criminal defendants. And because I’d been there — and because J. was my first client — keeping him out of prison and without a felony record had become my lodestar. But the facts were overwhelmingly against J. Mitigating evidence was the only thing that might persuade the judge and prosecutor that J. should get a break. I talked to J.’s mother, his teachers, the administrators at his school. They all told me he was charming and thoughtful, intelligent, though he rarely took the time to do his work and was often near trouble. But mostly I spent hours on the phone with the victim’s mother. By the end, she, too, was telling me that prison wasn’t where J. belonged.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">After months of conversation, the prosecutor’s stance toward J. softened. What began as a plea bargain for a sentence to be determined by the judge — a maximum of five years with the right to argue for a sentence of as little as six months — became an offer of time served plus three years of probation, in return for a guilty plea to a felony. He avoided a prison cell. We thought of it as a win. But I knew from experience what it meant to walk out of that courtroom with a felony conviction.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">On the morning of May 5, 2017, the Connecticut bar examiners released the results of the bar exam. Exactly 50 percent of those who took the test that day passed. When I checked the website, the names were listed in alphabetical order. The sixth entry from the top was Reginald Dwayne Betts. I stared at the name that I share with my father and took a deep breath. He never visited me in prison. I didn’t invite him to my wedding. He had missed four college graduations. And still, I thought of him first, our name a reminder of how success never erases history. Reginald Dwayne Betts, still connected to 251534, my state number. For so many years that number was more important than that name, and it might still be.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">About three months later, the letter came from the Connecticut Bar Examining Committee telling me that the committee needed more time to decide if I had the character and fitness required of a lawyer. The committee would continue reviewing my application. They might contact my references, ask me to appear at an investigative hearing, look into my life before and after my incarceration.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">Character and fitness, loosely defined qualities, are required of attorneys in every state. States take various approaches to determining if someone with a criminal record meets that standard and will become a licensed attorney. For those convicted of felonies in Florida, the governor must restore your civil rights by petition, a yearslong process, before you can sit for the bar exam. In Washington State, bar examiners must determine that a person has the appropriate character and fitness to practice law before they may sit for the bar exam. In Mississippi, for most felons, there is no record of rehabilitation; no stretch of time spent outside prison; no letter written by any defense attorney, prosecutor or judge; no prayer that will persuade the bar examiner to confer a law license on you. Texas, Kansas and Missouri require that five years pass after a felon finishes his prison sentence and/or probation time before he or she can apply for bar admission.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">Willie Dow, a local New Haven attorney, agreed to represent me. Soon after, Michael Wishnie, a Yale Law professor, volunteered to serve as co-counsel. We put together a packet of more than 200 letters from friends, colleagues and professors attesting to my character. The support was humbling, but it also felt strange to create an elaborate record to prove that I was worthy of doing a job that I was qualified to do. Once the packet was collected, Willie sent it to the Connecticut bar examiners, and we waited. I knew that if the committee declined to admit me to the bar, I would have to wait two years to reapply for admission, at which point I would probably have to take the bar exam again and also pay the $800 fee again. I knew that what I did as a 16-year-old in Virginia would forever be a hellhound on my trail, but I hoped that I had outpaced my scapegrace. The committee’s July letter let me know: not yet; maybe, not ever.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div class=""></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">When I despaired,</strong> I thought about Benjamin Franklin Rayborn. J. Edgar Hoover, the former director of the F.B.I., said that Rayborn was “the most notorious bank robber since World War II.” Upon his arrest, his local paper, The Courier-Journal of Louisville, Ky., on Dec. 18, 1949, called Rayborn “the trigger-happy fellow with the cold, blue eyes.” Before Rayborn turned 30, he had been sentenced to life in Kentucky for a string of bank robberies; sentenced to 30 years in federal prison for being caught in a hotel room full of machine guns; led a riot against prison conditions in Kentucky; and been transferred to Alcatraz, then one of the most violent prisons in America, as punishment for his role in the riot. But by 1956, Rayborn had fashioned himself into a legal mind astute enough to successfully challenge several federal convictions and get 10 years knocked off his federal prison sentence. His next legal feat was more astonishing. Once he seemed primed for an early release, Kentucky sought to have him extradited back to state prison to serve the rest of his life sentence. Rayborn again filed suit, arguing that Kentucky, by voluntarily transferring him to Alcatraz, had relinquished any jurisdictional claims it had to his body. A judge agreed. Finally free, Rayborn, according to an acquaintance, sought a job as an accountant at General Electric. After achieving a perfect score on an exam given by the company, he was asked where he had studied accounting. Alcatraz, he told them. That disqualified him. He went on to rob another bank and returned to federal prison.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">In 1966, John Cleary, the first executive director of the Federal Defenders of San Diego, met Benjamin Franklin Rayborn in prison in Atlanta. Cleary, impressed by Rayborn’s habeas corpus filings on behalf of other prisoners, helped him get grant money to be paid as a jailhouse lawyer. After his second release from prison, Cleary tapped Rayborn to work with the federal defenders. He called Rayborn a one-man appellate division. From 1971 until his death in 2004, Rayborn was the chief legal research associate. In his role, he wrote hundreds of appeal briefs and had his handprint on thousands of others. For three decades, one of the most brilliant legal minds, with a photographic memory and decades of doing time, ran the appellate division of the federal defenders. All told, he spent more than 20 years incarcerated and more than three decades on the job. Cleary called him among the top 1 percent of attorneys in the country, and he had never earned a law degree or been licensed to practice law.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">There are others. Frankie Guzman, found guilty at age 15 of armed robbery in a juvenile court, was admitted to the California Bar and is now director of the California Youth Justice Initiative at the National Center for Youth Law. Noah Kilroy, Christopher Poulos and Shon Hopwood have also traded in inmate numbers for bar numbers. Kilroy served time for drug charges in Florida. He works as a prosecutor for the City of Providence and has started his own criminal-defense practice. Poulos went from doing federal time for drug trafficking to working in President Obama’s White House for the Office of National Drug Control Policy; he’s now director of the Washington Statewide Re-entry Council.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">Hopwood might be the Rayborn of the group. While serving a 12-year sentence for bank robbery, he became a jailhouse lawyer and managed to get two cases in front of the United States Supreme Court. Now a professor at Georgetown Law, Hopwood recently represented Tarra Simmons before the Washington Supreme Court. The three years Simmons served in prison for drug charges almost kept her from becoming an attorney. The Washington Bar Examining Committee, citing character and fitness issues, wouldn’t permit her to take the bar exam. Arguing before the same body that admitted him, Hopwood helped make Simmons’s own legal career possible.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">Hopwood, Simmons, Poulos, Guzman and Kilroy are all licensed attorneys embodying the history of thousands of men and women standing for count. Their stories reminded me of my long-ago cellmate Keese, and I wondered what might be possible for him if he were set free. More than 20 years after a Richmond judge sentenced him to over five decades in prison, and with my own future as a lawyer uncertain at best, I managed to find three attorneys in Washington who agreed to rumble for Keese the way he had been ready to rumble for me. Maybe we all pursued law to save someone left behind; maybe we pursued it to save ourselves.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0"><strong class="css-8qgvsz ebyp5n10">Prison taught me</strong> how to wait. I learned to break up a nine-year sentence into a million moments of waiting: waiting for rec, for chow, for count time, waiting for mail call, for visits, waiting to walk in the world without thinking constantly about danger, waiting for freedom. Waiting to hear back from the bar committee felt similar — once again, I was just waiting for mail. When the letter came, it began: “Dear Mr. Betts: The Connecticut Bar Examining Committee is pleased to advise you ... that you have been recommended for admission to the Connecticut Bar.”</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">On Nov. 3, 2017, two days before my 37th birthday, my mother, along with my Aunt Pandora and Uncle Darren, who had driven more than 300 miles to watch it all, walked into the New Haven Superior Court on 235 Church Street. They deposited keys, watches, belts and cellphones into a gray bucket before walking through a metal detector. Minutes later, Terese, Micah, Miles and I followed them through the same gantlet. Most black families walking into this courthouse are headed into a disaster. But that day, my folks joined my friends and colleagues in a small fourth-floor courtroom to celebrate.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div><div class=""></div><div class="css-s99gbd StoryBodyCompanionColumn"><div class="css-53u6y8"><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">Inside, the deep, rich mahogany pews called to mind the Fairfax County courtroom where I learned I’d do time. Looking around, I thought about the squalor and violence of the prison cells. Minutes later, I stood before Judge Omar Williams with my friend and supervising attorney Sanford O. Bruce III, who goes by Trey. The swearing-in, for most, is simply a ritual; in courtrooms across the country, judges administer the oath to countless new attorneys each time bar results are released. But this was different. I stood alone before the judge, with just a lawyer to the right of me, as I’d done so many years before.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">Trey asked the court to approve my admission to the Connecticut Bar. “You’ve fought long and hard with the sword to get here today,” Judge Williams said, “and today you’re presented with the shield.” Nearly three years before that day, as a second-year law student at Yale, I walked into the office that Trey and Judge Williams, then a public defender, shared. I told Trey that I had a record and asked if it would be a problem for me to work in the Criminal Justice Clinic. He told me that if there was anyone who should be working in the clinic, it was someone like me, who from experience understood what the clinic’s clients were going through.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">Reciting the oath to become a member of the court took a fraction of the time that it took me to be sentenced. I wondered if becoming an attorney would fill the hole in my mother’s heart that my becoming a felon created. The courtroom was where she lost her son; maybe it would be where she got him back, too.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">I could see my mother wiping her eyes, but smiling this time. Someone clapped. My Uncle Darren had his phone out, recording. Terese, who had been there long before any of this was a possibility, was beaming, her arms wrapped around Micah and Miles. Everyone there knew that I once walked out of a courtroom headed to prison, except my younger son, Miles, who was 6. Terese and I hadn’t decided when we would let Miles know. But we hoped that it would be on our terms and not because of a classmate or an overheard conversation.</p><p class="css-axufdj evys1bk0">After the 15-minute ceremony, I turned to everyone, without thinking, and began speaking as my uncle kept recording: “The last time my mom saw me in court, I was sentenced to nine years in prison.” I wanted to say something about the journey. I’d already revealed too much. Miles, sitting beside his brother, paused and looked up. I could tell something confused him. He had questions in his eyes. He stared, listening, as I confessed the thing that I’d been holding back. What man wants to tell his child he’s done time in prison? But I had. And, in that single breath, I’d given him this: an image of his father as both a convict and an attorney.</p></div><aside class="css-ew4tgv" aria-label="companion column"></aside></div></section><div></div><div></div><div></div><div></div><div><div id="bottom-wrapper" class="css-2fttua"><div id="bottom-slug" class="css-l9onyx"><p>Advertisement</p></div><a href="#after-bottom" class="css-1ly73wi">Continue reading the main story</a><div class="ad bottom-wrapper css-rfqw0c" id="bottom" style="min-height:90px"></div><div {"initialState":{"Article:QXJ0aWNsZTpueXQ6Ly9hcnRpY2xlLzQ5NjlhMzgyLWYwZjYtNWM2OC05ZDY3LTg2ZGIxMzNlZjAxYQ==":{"__typename":"Article","id":"QXJ0aWNsZTpueXQ6Ly9hcnRpY2xlLzQ5NjlhMzgyLWYwZjYtNWM2OC05ZDY3LTg2ZGIxMzNlZjAxYQ==","compatibility":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Article:QXJ0aWNsZTpueXQ6Ly9hcnRpY2xlLzQ5NjlhMzgyLWYwZjYtNWM2OC05ZDY3LTg2ZGIxMzNlZjAxYQ==.compatibility","typename":"CompatibilityFeatures"},"archiveProperties":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Article:QXJ0aWNsZTpueXQ6Ly9hcnRpY2xlLzQ5NjlhMzgyLWYwZjYtNWM2OC05ZDY3LTg2ZGIxMzNlZjAxYQ==.archiveProperties","typename":"ArticleArchiveProperties"},"collections@filterEmpty":[{"type":"id","generated":false,"id":"LegacyCollection:TGVnYWN5Q29sbGVjdGlvbjpueXQ6Ly9sZWdhY3ljb2xsZWN0aW9uL2U2ZGRhNzZhLTlmMjMtNWVmNy05MzZmLTFkMmNjNjlmZjVmMA==","typename":"LegacyCollection"},{"type":"id","generated":false,"id":"LegacyCollection:TGVnYWN5Q29sbGVjdGlvbjpueXQ6Ly9sZWdhY3ljb2xsZWN0aW9uLzExZjcyYWI0LTdjZDAtNTQwYS05M2NjLWYzNWIzMmNkMDEzZA==","typename":"LegacyCollection"},{"type":"id","generated":false,"id":"LegacyCollection:TGVnYWN5Q29sbGVjdGlvbjpueXQ6Ly9sZWdhY3ljb2xsZWN0aW9uL2YyYjJjZTRkLWY0N2EtNTQ5MS05ZmE2LWVhOTdlZDA0YmEwZA==","typename":"LegacyCollection"},{"type":"id","generated":false,"id":"LegacyCollection:TGVnYWN5Q29sbGVjdGlvbjpueXQ6Ly9sZWdhY3ljb2xsZWN0aW9uLzIzMjExNzM4LTRmYmQtNWRjOC05OTRlLTk4ZTY4NTAzYTA3Mw==","typename":"LegacyCollection"}],"tone":"FEATURE","section":{"type":"id","generated":false,"id":"Section:U2VjdGlvbjpueXQ6Ly9zZWN0aW9uL2E5MTNkMWZiLTNjZGYtNTU2Yi05YTgxLWYwYjk5NmExYTIwMg==","typename":"Section"},"subsection":null,"sprinkledBody":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Article:QXJ0aWNsZTpueXQ6Ly9hcnRpY2xlLzQ5NjlhMzgyLWYwZjYtNWM2OC05ZDY3LTg2ZGIxMzNlZjAxYQ==.sprinkledBody","typename":"DocumentBlock"},"url":"http:\u002F\u002Fweb.archive.org\u002Fweb\u002F20211230230233\u002Fhttps:\u002F\u002Fwww.nytimes.com\u002F2018\u002F10\u002F16\u002Fmagazine\u002Ffelon-attorney-crime-yale-law.html","adTargetingParams({\"c everyone.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.0.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.0.timecode":{"start":0.5,"end":2.15,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.1":{"text":"My name is Reginald Dwayne Betts, and a few years ago I wrote a piece for The New York Times magazine about my life.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.1.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.1.timecode":{"start":2.15,"end":11.6,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.2":{"text":"It was about going to prison at 16 and coming home, and then my attempts at becoming an attorney.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.2.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.2.timecode":{"start":11.6,"end":19.85,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.3":{"text":"And all of it has come back to me more recently and reminded me what the piece was really about.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.3.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.3.timecode":{"start":19.85,"end":28.32,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.4":{"text":"I was listening to my oldest son, Micah, talk to his classmates, and I heard him say, \"As you all know, my father went to jail.\"","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.4.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.4.timecode":{"start":28.32,"end":38.32,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.5":{"text":"And the class, they were talking about the death of George Floyd, and what my son was asking was why prison was the place for 16-year-old me.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.5.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.5.timecode":{"start":38.32,"end":50.61,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.6":{"text":"And why prison would have been a place, had Floyd gotten arrested and convicted of passing a fake $20 bill, prison would have been the place for him.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.6.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.6.timecode":{"start":50.61,"end":62.63,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.7":{"text":"And why that same place will be the place for, if convicted, the officer that killed Floyd.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.7.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.7.timecode":{"start":62.63,"end":71.34,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.8":{"text":"And I think what he was asking is just: What is punishment in America?","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.8.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.8.timecode":{"start":71.34,"end":78.1,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.9":{"text":"What is it for?","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.9.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.9.timecode":{"start":78.1,"end":79.24,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.10":{"text":"And how should we think about it?","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.10.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.10.timecode":{"start":79.24,"end":81.31,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.11":{"text":"And the part about it all that encouraged me, I think, is that listening to my son, I can hear that he wasn't ashamed of what I did.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.11.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.11.timecode":{"start":81.31,"end":90,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.12":{"text":"He recognized it was wrong, but the hurt really came from knowing that he lives in a society that imagined prison was the place for me.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.12.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.12.timecode":{"start":90,"end":100.88,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.13":{"text":"And maybe from living in a society that imagines prison is the place for anybody.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.13.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.13.timecode":{"start":100.88,"end":115.21,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.14":{"text":"So here's my piece, \"Getting Out,\" read by J. D. Jackson.","speaker":"REGINALD DWAYNE BETTS","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.14.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.14.timecode":{"start":115.21,"end":118.97,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.15":{"text":"One afternoon in the fall of 2016, I sat in a windowless visiting room at the Manson Youth Institution in Cheshire, Conn.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.15.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.15.timecode":{"start":118.97,"end":135.69,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.16":{"text":"A recent graduate of Yale Law School, I was a certified legal intern on a fellowship in the New Haven public defender's office.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.16.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.16.timecode":{"start":135.69,"end":143.97,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.17":{"text":"J., a lanky 18-year-old brown-skinned kid sitting across from me, was my first client.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.17.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.17.timecode":{"start":143.97,"end":150.83,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.18":{"text":"He didn't talk.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.18.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.18.timecode":{"start":150.83,"end":152.69,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.19":{"text":"Instead he stared at me as if I were the police.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.19.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.19.timecode":{"start":152.69,"end":156.55,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.20":{"text":"Sanford O. Bruce III, my supervising attorney, listened as I explained to J. (one of his initials) what we knew of the charges against him.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.20.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.20.timecode":{"start":156.55,"end":166.36,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.21":{"text":"A young man with whom J. attended high school had claimed that J. and another kid he didn't know had threatened him with a pistol, then robbed him of his cellphone and a couple of hundred dollars.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.21.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.21.timecode":{"start":166.36,"end":177.7,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.22":{"text":"Officers arrested J. minutes later, but the other suspect, who supposedly held the gun, was never found.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.22.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.22.timecode":{"start":177.7,"end":185.58,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.23":{"text":"The prosecutor thought he should serve time in prison.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.23.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.23.timecode":{"start":185.58,"end":188.45,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.24":{"text":"I let J. know this and described what would happen next: a series of court dates, a bond-reduction motion, plea-bargain offers.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.24.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.24.timecode":{"start":188.45,"end":198.37,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.25":{"text":"After remaining silent for nearly 40 minutes, he leaned forward in the blue plastic chair, cutting me off, and asked, \"Aren't you the one who did time in prison?\"","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.25.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.25.timecode":{"start":198.37,"end":209.83,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.26":{"text":"With a single question, this kid reminded me of what a law degree, even one from Yale, could not do -- make my own criminal history vanish.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.26.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.26.timecode":{"start":213.16,"end":224.74,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.27":{"text":"On Dec.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.27.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.27.timecode":{"start":224.74,"end":226.26,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.28":{"text":"7, 1996, a month and two days after my 16th birthday, I climbed with four other people into a beat-up ink-colored sedan in Prince George's County, Md.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.28.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.28.timecode":{"start":226.26,"end":237.45,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.29":{"text":"During that year, I'd read the Evelyn Wood guide to speed reading and J. California Cooper's novel \"The Wake of the Wind.\"","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.29.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.29.timecode":{"start":237.45,"end":246.16,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.30":{"text":"My Advanced Placement U.S. history teacher at Suitland High School had nicknamed me Smoky after he spied me rolling a blunt before his first-period class.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.30.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.30.timecode":{"start":246.16,"end":255.49,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.31":{"text":"I hadn't won a fight since second grade.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.31.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.31.timecode":{"start":255.49,"end":258.6,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.32":{"text":"Had been suspended half a dozen times -- once for setting off a stink bomb, but every other time for what teachers called being disruptive but was really just talking too much.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.32.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.32.timecode":{"start":258.6,"end":270.75,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.33":{"text":"People knew me for finding four-leaf clovers, doing back flips and making too many jokes.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.33.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.33.timecode":{"start":270.75,"end":277.74,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.34":{"text":"I didn't know who I was.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.34.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.34.timecode":{"start":277.74,"end":279.365,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.35":{"text":"The driver, who was in his early 20s, was a stranger to me.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.35.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.35.timecode":{"start":282.86,"end":286.88,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.36":{"text":"I half-knew his cousin.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.36.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.36.timecode":{"start":286.88,"end":288.89,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.37":{"text":"I'd brought my friend Marcus, who had played on the junior-varsity basketball team with me, to the cousin's house to get high.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.37.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.37.timecode":{"start":288.89,"end":296.5,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.38":{"text":"In the passenger seat, another face I'd just met said a robbery would pad our pockets.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.38.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.38.timecode":{"start":296.5,"end":303.9,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.39":{"text":"Weed, ignorance and a desire for a come-up, wanting just a few more dollars than we had, made us believe him.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.39.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.39.timecode":{"start":303.9,"end":313.52,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.40":{"text":"We ended up on I-95 at night, headed to the Springfield Mall in Fairfax County, Va.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.40.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.40.timecode":{"start":313.52,"end":319.68,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.41":{"text":"The driver gave me his pistol -- because I'd asked for it.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.41.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.41.timecode":{"start":319.68,"end":324.62,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.42":{"text":"I stared at it, my life about to be riven.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.42.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.42.timecode":{"start":324.62,"end":330.5,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.43":{"text":"He told me not to take the gun's safety off, for any reason.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.43.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.43.timecode":{"start":330.5,"end":334.07,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.44":{"text":"An accidental gunshot could get us all killed or sent to prison for life.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.44.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.44.timecode":{"start":334.07,"end":338.95,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.45":{"text":"At the mall, I first approached two people headed toward a parked car.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.45.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.45.timecode":{"start":338.95,"end":343.15,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.46":{"text":"My arm stretched toward the ground, the pistol dangling in my hand.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.46.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.46.timecode":{"start":343.15,"end":348.66,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.47":{"text":"One of them turned and saw me.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.47.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.47.timecode":{"start":348.66,"end":351.04,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.48":{"text":"Before I knew it, two women jumped in their car and disappeared.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.48.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.48.timecode":{"start":351.04,"end":356.5,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.49":{"text":"Minutes later, I approached one of the few cars in the lot.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.49.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.49.timecode":{"start":356.5,"end":360.45,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.50":{"text":"I saw a man asleep.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.50.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.50.timecode":{"start":360.45,"end":362.61,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.51":{"text":"I tapped twice on the window with the pistol's barrel, demanded that he get out of the car and turn over his keys and his wallet.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.51.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.51.timecode":{"start":362.61,"end":369.67,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.52":{"text":"Marcus and I drove away in the stolen vehicle.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.52.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.52.timecode":{"start":369.67,"end":373,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.53":{"text":"The next day, before the end of the Sunday church service Marcus's mother attended, the police had us in handcuffs, caught at a different Virginia mall shopping with another man's credit card.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.53.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.53.timecode":{"start":373,"end":387.63,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.54":{"text":"After meeting with J., I sat in my office with his file, a thin sheaf of no more than a dozen pages.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.54.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.54.timecode":{"start":387.63,"end":395.94,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.55":{"text":"I pulled out my own criminal record.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.55.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.55.timecode":{"start":395.94,"end":399,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.56":{"text":"One hundred-odd pages spilled from the accordion folder.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.56.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.56.timecode":{"start":399,"end":403.48,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.57":{"text":"I stared at the documents scattered across my desk: my neat cursive on the handwritten confession; a faded yellow summons demanding my mother appear in court, as if my crime belonged to her; the sentencing order consigning me to prison.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.57.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.57.timecode":{"start":403.48,"end":420.54,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.58":{"text":"The prison scrubs that J. wore, the jail cell that held him, the early mornings when deputies would take him shackled and cuffed to court, connected us.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.58.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.58.timecode":{"start":420.54,"end":432.38,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.59":{"text":"I wanted him to believe that the worst of what might happen could be overcome.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.59.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.59.timecode":{"start":432.38,"end":438.44,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.60":{"text":"But I wasn't sure if that was true.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.60.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.60.timecode":{"start":438.44,"end":442.98,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.61":{"text":"Some 10 months later, I would receive a letter from the Connecticut Bar Examining Committee.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.61.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.61.timecode":{"start":442.98,"end":448.55,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.62":{"text":"The committee, it said, would not recommend me for admission to the bar.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.62.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.62.timecode":{"start":448.55,"end":453.38,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.63":{"text":"Under Connecticut law, felons are presumed to lack the character and fitness required to practice law unless they can prove otherwise.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.63.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.63.timecode":{"start":453.38,"end":462.88,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.64":{"text":"I might eventually be allowed to practice law, or, I realized with a cold, dull clarity, I might not.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.64.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.64.timecode":{"start":462.88,"end":471.87,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.65":{"text":"One time in prison, I watched a man crack the jaw of another man with a padlock rolled up in a sock.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.65.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.65.timecode":{"start":474.62,"end":481.84,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.66":{"text":"Everyone knew the victim as the man who made wine by letting bread, fruit and jelly ferment in a black trash bag.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.66.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.66.timecode":{"start":481.84,"end":489.49,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.67":{"text":"He brought happiness and escape to the men there.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.67.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.67.timecode":{"start":489.49,"end":493.11,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.68":{"text":"When he was struck, blood squirted from his nose and mouth.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.68.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.68.timecode":{"start":493.11,"end":498.25,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.69":{"text":"He seemed startled that such pain had found him, astonished that he had been hurt in that way.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.69.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.69.timecode":{"start":498.25,"end":504.75,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.70":{"text":"But me, I'd known what to expect before the disappointing news from the bar examiners arrived.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.70.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.70.timecode":{"start":504.75,"end":512.22,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.71":{"text":"And still, the letter left me weeping.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.71.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.71.timecode":{"start":512.22,"end":516.03,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.72":{"text":"Months after pleading guilty to carjacking, attempted robbery and a firearm charge, I sat shackled and cuffed in the Fairfax County Courthouse, waiting for my sentencing.","speaker":"J. D. JACKSON","timecode":{"type":"id","generated":true,"id":"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.72.timecode","typename":"AudioTimecode"},"__typename":"AudioTranscriptFragment"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.72.timecode":{"start":519.32,"end":530.25,"__typename":"AudioTimecode"},"$Audio:QXVkaW86bnl0Oi8vYXVkaW8vNjAwM2RjODktNDgyNy01ZWMyLWJmOTktOTgxYTg1NzhjY2Ri.transcript.transcriptFragment.73":{"text":"I faced a maximum of life plus 13 years.","speaker":"J. D.
